claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and any children you're responsible for.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK. I have two children - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14. I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know you have two children - Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14) - and that you live in the UK.

Now I need to understand your living situation with your children:

**Do you live with both Alex and Blake, or do either of them live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home, but Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives elsewhere.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So Blake lives with you, but Alex lives elsewhere.

I need to understand more about Alex's situation:

**Who does Alex live with, and what financial contributions, if any, are you making towards Alex's upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or housing)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner. I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep though - I help with things like clothes, food, and other essentials. I send money regularly to help support them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To determine if you can continue claiming Child Benefit for Alex beyond the initial 8 weeks, I need to understand the value of your contributions:

**What is the approximate amount you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week or month? This will help me assess if your contributions match or exceed the Child Benefit payment amount.**

(For reference, Child Benefit rates vary depending on whether it's your first child or additional children, but this will help me evaluate your situation.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now let me ask about Blake:

**Are you responsible for Blake's care and looking after them - meaning you live with Blake and are providing for their needs like food, clothes, and other essentials?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Blake lives with me and I provide for all their needs - food, clothes, everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. I have one more important question about Blake:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an apprenticeship), or have they left education/training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is 14, so they're still in school - just doing their regular secondary school studies.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for that information. I now have all the details I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both children.

Let me review your situation:

- **Alex (age 4)**: Lives with your ex-partner, but you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep
- **Blake (age 14)**: Lives with you and you provide for all their needs; they're in secondary school

Based on the Child Benefit eligibility rules, let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! You are **eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

**Summary:**

- **Alex (age 4)**: You are eligible because you're making regular contributions of £50 per week towards their upkeep, even though they live with your ex-partner. This meets the requirement for being responsible for a child who doesn't live with you.

- **Blake (age 14)**: You are eligible because you live with Blake, provide for all their needs, and they're in secondary school education.

**Important note:** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. If your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would need to contact them to discuss who should claim, or you can contact HMRC to sort this out. For Blake, make sure no one else is claiming for them either.

If you'd like to proceed with a claim, you can apply through the official Child Benefit application process with HMRC.

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you, but you are making regular financial contributions of £50 per week towards their upkeep (food, clothes, and other essentials). According to the rules, you can get Child Benefit for a child who doesn't live with you if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care. You are therefore responsible for Alex and eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

Blake is 14 years old, lives with you, and you provide for all their needs. Since Blake is under 16 and you live with them and are responsible for their care, you qualify for Child Benefit. Additionally, Blake is in approved education (secondary school), which further supports eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake. For Alex, despite them living with your ex-partner, your regular weekly contributions of £50 towards their upkeep meet the threshold for claiming. For Blake, you meet the standard eligibility criteria as you live with them, provide for their needs, and they are in secondary school education. However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child - if your ex-partner is also claiming for Alex, you would need to agree between yourselves who should claim, or HMRC will decide. For Blake, ensure no one else is claiming for them.

According to the rules, you can get Child Benefit for a child who doesn't live with you if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care. You are therefore responsible for Alex and eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

Blake is 14 years old, lives with you, and you provide for all their needs. Since Blake is under 16 and you live with them and are responsible for their care, you qualify for Child Benefit. Additionally, Blake is in approved education (secondary school), which further supports eligibility.

For Alex, despite them living with your ex-partner, your regular weekly contributions of £50 towards their upkeep meet the threshold for claiming. For Blake, you meet the standard eligibility criteria as you live with them, provide for their needs, and they are in secondary school education. However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child - if your ex-partner is also claiming for Alex, you would need to agree between yourselves who should claim, or HMRC will decide. For Blake, ensure no one else is claiming for them.
